title: Canonical IDs collide across substrates — same prefix (FEAT-/TASK-/DEC-/FGAP-/etc.) numbered from 001 in each substrate produces ambiguous references when work spans both
description: Each substrate directory (.project, .context-jit-spec-v2, future per-arc substrates) maintains its own ID namespace for items in each block kind. Both .project and .context-jit-spec-v2 start FEAT-NNN numbering at FEAT-001 and grow independently. Lived evidence 2026-05-30 this session: .project/relations.json has 3 edges touching an entity named FEAT-005 (referenced by DEC-0044 / DEC-0047 / FEAT-010); .context-jit-spec-v2/features.json has its own FEAT-005 (author-agent-spec correctness arc filed during the per-arc PM decomposition). These are DIFFERENT items in DIFFERENT substrates that share the same canonical ID. A reference to FEAT-005 in conversation, in a commit message, in an analysis document, in cross-substrate edges, or in agent prompts is genuinely ambiguous — the reader cannot determine which entity is meant without external substrate-naming context. The cross-substrate sentinel form (substrate-name:ID, e.g. project:FGAP-115) exists at the relations.json edge layer and was used during this session's filing of FB-NNN to project:FGAP-NNN edges, but the same disambiguation has no canonical surface at conversational / commit-message / analysis-document / agent-prompt layers. Per-arc substrate engagement pattern (the design pattern that produced .context-jit-spec-v2 alongside .project) makes the collision systemic — every per-arc substrate created from now forward inherits the same ambiguous-ID-without-substrate-namespace concern.

evidence.reference: 3 edges touching 'FEAT-005' as parent or child: DEC-0044 → FEAT-005 (decision_addresses_feature); DEC-0047 → FEAT-005 (decision_addresses_feature); FEAT-010 → FEAT-005 (feature_depends_on_item). These reference .project's FEAT-005 (canonical operational substrate's fifth feature).
FEAT-005 entry: 'author-agent-spec correctness arc' filed 2026-05-30 during per-arc PM decomposition. This is a DIFFERENT entity than .project's FEAT-005.

Multi-turn analysis built on the implicit assumption that 'FEAT-005' was unambiguously the per-arc-substrate entity. The collision surfaced when the user prompted 'did you look in .project' and direct query revealed the .project FEAT-005 referenced by 3 unrelated edges.

Per-arc substrate engagement (the design pattern of creating .context-<arc-name>/ per significant design or implementation arc) will produce additional substrates each with their own FEAT-/TASK-/DEC-/etc. namespaces. Every arc inherits the same collision.

impact: Cross-substrate work becomes ambiguous at every reference layer that isn't a relations.json edge. Concrete: (a) commit messages referencing 'FEAT-005' don't say which substrate; (b) analysis documents referencing canonical IDs across substrates have to either spell out the substrate every time or risk misinterpretation; (c) agent prompts that pass canonical IDs as inputs cannot reliably resolve them without substrate context; (d) orchestrator conversation about cross-substrate state risks the kind of mis-identification this session experienced (multi-turn analysis of an entity from the wrong substrate); (e) any future cross-substrate consultation tool (companion to /context switch family per the design space) inherits the disambiguation requirement at every API boundary; (f) the orchestration-contract assertion 'real-check disproves wrong claims' is undermined when the wrong-claim derives from collision-induced mis-identification rather than from a verifiable substrate-state question.
canonical_vocabulary: ID namespace collision; cross-substrate reference ambiguity; substrate-namespaced ID; sentinel form (substrate-name:ID); per-arc substrate engagement pattern; canonical ID resolution context
proposed_resolution:
Operator decides resolution among canonical pi-context patterns. Three load-bearing requirements regardless of selected mechanism: (R1) every cross-substrate reference (commit message, analysis doc, agent prompt, cross-substrate edge, orchestrator conversation) carries unambiguous substrate disambiguation; (R2) the disambiguation mechanism is uniform across all reference layers (relations.json edges + conversation + commits + prompts) so a reader does not need different parsing rules per layer; (R3) the mechanism is queryable from substrate metadata so an agent can resolve a substrate-qualified ID to its canonical home without out-of-band knowledge.

Mechanism options grounded in pi-context canon:

(M1) Substrate-prefix ID expansion at filing time: when a new per-arc substrate is bootstrapped, its config declares an id_prefix_modifier (e.g. spec-v2:) that is prepended to every canonical ID at filing time. .context-jit-spec-v2's FEAT-005 becomes spec-v2:FEAT-005 in its own substrate's features.json id field. Universal disambiguation at the data layer. Breaks existing per-substrate ID patterns; cost is migration of every existing per-arc filing (currently small — .context-jit-spec-v2 is the only per-arc substrate; ~70 items).

(M2) Substrate-qualified canonical reference convention at the consumption layer: substrate IDs remain unqualified internally; cross-substrate references ALWAYS use sentinel form substrate-name:ID per the pattern already established in this session's FB→project:FGAP edges. Extend to commit messages + analysis docs + orchestrator conversation as documented convention. Lower cost (no migration); higher discipline-load (every reference must remember to qualify).

(M3) Substrate-namespaced display rendering: keep raw IDs unqualified in the JSON; rendering layers (read-block tool output, /context view lens output, conversational reports) always emit substrate-qualified form. Reader sees 'spec-v2:FEAT-005' always; substrate JSON stays clean. Requires every read surface to participate; missed surfaces leak ambiguity.

Recommendation: (M2) — lowest cost, builds on existing sentinel-form pattern, makes the discipline explicit at the consumption layer where ambiguity surfaces (not at the storage layer where IDs are unambiguous within their substrate). Add a per-arc invariant that asserts: for every substrate-qualified reference appearing in a per-arc substrate's items (e.g. FB→project:FGAP edges), the qualifier substring (e.g. project:) appears in a registered list of known substrate names — catches typos and missing qualifications structurally.

The ambiguity does NOT manifest within a single substrate (where IDs are unique by construction); it manifests at cross-substrate boundaries. Solution focuses on disambiguation at boundary-crossing references, not on universal renumbering.
